title: Thai Green Curry with Homemade Paste
nativeTitle: แกงเขียวหวาน
description: Thailand's beloved green curry built from a freshly ground paste of green chillies, lemongrass and kaffir lime, then simmered in rich coconut milk with chicken and handfuls of Thai basil. The colour comes from fresh rather than dried chillies, giving the sauce a grassy, aromatic heat that distinguishes it from every other curry on the table.

ingredients.name: boneless chicken thighs, cut into 3 cm pieces
coconut milk, full-fat
ingredients.notes: 400 ml; do not shake — the thick cream on top is used first

ingredients.name: fresh green chillies (bird's eye or serrano)
lemongrass stalks, tender white part only
kaffir lime leaves
ingredients.notes: 4 go into the paste; 4 torn for cooking

ingredients.name: galangal or fresh ginger, thinly sliced
ingredients.notes: galangal is traditional; fresh ginger is a fine substitute

ingredients.name: shrimp paste
ingredients.notes: omit to keep it pescatarian-friendly

ingredients.name: Thai basil or holy basil leaves
ingredients.notes: fresh tulsi is botanically close and works well

steps.text: Blend the green chillies, lemongrass, 4 kaffir lime leaves, galangal (or ginger), garlic, cumin, coriander seeds and shrimp paste (if using) with 3 tablespoons of water until a smooth, fragrant paste forms. A blender gives the finest result; a food processor or mixie also works. Set aside.

Open the coconut milk without shaking it. Spoon the thick cream off the top into a cold wok or kadai — you should get about 5-6 tablespoons. Heat over medium-high flame until the cream bubbles and sizzles, about 2-3 minutes.

Add all the green curry paste to the bubbling coconut cream. Stir-fry continuously on medium-high heat for 3-4 minutes until the paste darkens slightly, turns fragrant and the oil begins to separate at the edges — this step is essential to remove the raw taste.

Add the chicken pieces and toss to coat in the paste. Stir-fry on high heat for 3 minutes until the outside of the chicken is sealed and coloured, though it will still be raw inside — that is expected at this stage.
Pour in the remaining coconut milk and 100 ml of water. Add the fish sauce, jaggery and the remaining 4 torn kaffir lime leaves. Stir everything together and bring to a gentle simmer.

Cover and simmer on low-medium heat for 15 minutes, stirring once or twice, until the chicken is fully cooked through and the sauce has thickened slightly. Taste — it should be creamy, mildly sweet and fragrant. Adjust fish sauce for saltiness and jaggery for sweetness.
Turn off the heat and stir in the Thai basil or tulsi leaves. The residual heat wilts the leaves and releases their aroma without destroying it. Serve immediately over steamed jasmine rice.

substitutions.substitute: 400 g firm tofu or mixed vegetables (brinjal, zucchini, baby corn)
substitutions.notes: reduce simmering time to 8 minutes for tofu

substitutions.substitute: 2 tbsp light soy sauce plus a pinch of salt
substitutions.notes: loses some depth but still tasty

substitutions.substitute: 1 tsp brown sugar or regular sugar
1 tsp finely grated lime or lemon zest
substitutions.notes: add at the same stage, a little goes a long way

culturalNote: Green curry — gang kiao wan, meaning 'sweet green curry' — is one of Thailand's most iconic dishes, the colour and freshness of its paste a marker of how recently it was ground. Thai street vendors and home cooks alike consider making the paste from scratch a mark of care.
regionalVariation: Central Thailand keeps the paste thin and the gravy loose; northern Thailand makes a drier version with less coconut milk and more galangal, closer in spirit to a dry stir-fry.
indianKitchenAdaptation: A kadai works perfectly in place of a wok — high heat and a heavy base are all that matter. Galangal is sold as 'Thai adrak' in some metro specialty stores (Chennai, Mumbai, Bengaluru); fresh ginger (adrak) is an excellent everyday substitute. Holy basil (tulsi) plants are found in most Indian homes and work well in place of Thai basil. Full-fat coconut milk cans are widely available on BigBasket and Amazon India. If fish sauce is unavailable, use 2 tbsp soy sauce and a pinch of salt.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Thai Red Curry with Tofu and Vegetables
description: A deeply aromatic Thai red curry built on a vibrant paste of dried red chillies, lemongrass and galangal, simmered in coconut milk with golden tofu, brinjal and capsicum. Lighter in heat than the green version but equally perfumed, it is a weeknight-friendly vegan dish that improves as it rests.

ingredients.name: coconut milk, full-fat
ingredients.notes: 400 ml; do not shake

ingredients.name: dried red chillies, soaked in hot water 10 min
kaffir lime leaves
ingredients.notes: 3 for paste, 3 torn for cooking

ingredients.name: capsicum (red or green bell pepper), sliced
Thai basil or holy basil leaves

steps.text: Drain the soaked red chillies and blend with lemongrass, 3 kaffir lime leaves, galangal or ginger, garlic, cumin and coriander seeds with 3 tablespoons of water until a smooth, dark red paste forms. Set aside.

Press the tofu firmly between layers of paper towel or a clean cloth for 5 minutes to remove excess water, then cut into 2 cm cubes. This prevents splattering when you pan-fry and helps the tofu hold its shape in the curry.

Heat 1 tablespoon oil in a non-stick pan on medium-high heat. Pan-fry the tofu cubes for 4-5 minutes, turning once or twice, until golden on most sides. Remove and set aside — they will finish cooking in the curry.

In the same pan, heat remaining oil and spoon off the thick coconut cream from the top of the can. Fry the cream over medium-high heat for 2 minutes until it bubbles and separates slightly. Add the red curry paste and stir-fry for 3-4 minutes until dark, fragrant and the paste no longer smells raw.
Add the brinjal and capsicum and stir to coat in the paste. Pour in the remaining coconut milk, add soy sauce, jaggery and the remaining 3 torn kaffir lime leaves. Stir everything together.

Simmer uncovered on medium heat for 10-12 minutes until the vegetables are tender and the sauce is slightly thickened. Add the pan-fried tofu in the last 3 minutes so it warms through without breaking apart. Taste and adjust soy sauce and jaggery.
Turn off the heat and stir in the basil leaves. Serve hot over jasmine rice — the curry tastes even better the next day once the flavours have melded.

substitutions.substitute: 350 g mixed vegetables (mushroom, broccoli, baby corn)
substitutions.notes: reduce simmering time to 8 minutes

substitutions.substitute: coconut aminos for a soy-free version
1 tsp brown sugar
zucchini or mushrooms
substitutions.notes: any firm vegetable works well

culturalNote: Red curry paste gets its deep colour from dried red chillies rather than fresh, making it more shelf-stable than green curry paste and traditionally associated with the central plains of Thailand where dried spices were more common.
regionalVariation: Southern Thai red curry is sharper and spicier with extra dried chillies and turmeric; Chiang Mai-style versions use bamboo shoots and minimal coconut milk.
indianKitchenAdaptation: Use a kadai or non-stick pan. Dried Kashmiri red chillies or Byadgi chillies work beautifully in the paste — they give colour without overwhelming heat. Tofu (also known as 'bean curd' or 'tau fu') is available in the refrigerated section of large Indian supermarkets and organic stores. All other ingredients are readily sourced at Indian grocery stores or online.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Pad Thai with Prawns and Peanuts
description: Thailand's definitive street noodle — flat rice noodles tossed on furious heat with plump prawns, scrambled egg, crunchy bean sprouts and a tangy tamarind sauce, then blanketed in crushed peanuts and finished with a wedge of lime. Street food made at home in under 40 minutes.

ingredients.name: flat rice noodles, 3-5 mm width
ingredients.notes: soak in cold water 20 minutes until pliable, then drain

ingredients.name: spring onions, cut into 3 cm pieces
tamarind pulp, dissolved in 4 tbsp warm water and strained
lime or lemon wedges, to serve
dried red chilli flakes, to garnish

steps.text: Soak the rice noodles in cold water for 20 minutes until they are pliable and bend without snapping but are not fully soft — they will finish cooking in the wok. Drain and set aside. Meanwhile, mix the tamarind liquid, fish sauce and sugar in a small bowl to make the pad Thai sauce.
Heat a wok or large kadai on the highest flame your stove allows. Add 2 tablespoons of oil and, when it shimmers, add the minced garlic. Stir-fry for 20 seconds until lightly golden.

Add the prawns and stir-fry for 2 minutes until they turn pink and curl. Push them to the side of the wok, add the remaining tablespoon of oil to the centre, crack in the eggs and scramble them lightly. When the eggs are half-set, fold everything together.

Add the drained noodles to the wok and pour the pad Thai sauce over them immediately. Toss everything together on high heat for 2-3 minutes, using tongs or two spatulas, until the noodles absorb the sauce and pick up a little colour at the edges.
Add the bean sprouts and the white parts of the spring onion. Toss once or twice — 30 seconds maximum — to keep the sprouts crunchy. Taste and adjust fish sauce for salt or sugar for sweetness.

Plate immediately. Scatter crushed peanuts and the green parts of the spring onion over the top. Serve with a lime wedge for squeezing and a pinch of chilli flakes on the side for those who want more heat.

substitutions.substitute: 200 g chicken strips or firm tofu for non-seafood versions
substitutions.notes: chicken needs 4 minutes; tofu should be pressed and pan-fried first

substitutions.substitute: 2.5 tbsp soy sauce plus a pinch of salt
substitutions.notes: slightly less complex but works well

substitutions.substitute: 2 tbsp lime juice plus 1 tsp brown sugar
substitutions.notes: less deep but tangy

substitutions.substitute: roasted cashew pieces or toasted sesame seeds for peanut allergy
substitutions.notes: cashews give a richer mouthfeel

culturalNote: Pad Thai became Thailand's national noodle dish partly through a 1940s government campaign to promote rice noodles over wheat noodles and unify a sense of Thai identity — a rare example of politics shaping a country's most beloved street food.
regionalVariation: Bangkok street stalls serve it drier, with the egg cooked in a thin omelette wrapper around the noodles; Chiang Mai versions often use thin rice vermicelli instead of flat noodles.
indianKitchenAdaptation: High heat is the soul of Pad Thai — a cast-iron kadai on a gas burner works perfectly. Use your stove's largest burner at full power. Flat rice noodles (called 'rice noodles' or 'banh pho' in Indian stores) are sold in most large supermarkets and online. Tamarind pulp (imli) is at every Indian grocery store; dissolve a small ball the size of a cherry in warm water and strain. Fish sauce is available at select gourmet stores and on Amazon India; soy sauce is a straightforward substitute.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Thai Pad Krapow (Holy Basil Chicken Stir-Fry)
description: The quintessential Thai street-food fix — minced chicken stir-fried at fierce heat with garlic, fresh chillies, fish sauce and oyster sauce, then lifted by an avalanche of holy basil. Spooned over jasmine rice and crowned with a runny-yolk fried egg, it is ready in under 25 minutes and endlessly satisfying.

ingredients.name: holy basil or Thai basil leaves (krapow)
ingredients.notes: about 25-30 leaves; fresh tulsi is an excellent substitute

steps.text: Using the flat of a knife or a small mortar and pestle, pound the garlic and green chillies into a rough paste — leave some texture and visible chilli seeds rather than grinding it smooth. This coarse paste fries better and gives more character than a blender paste.

Heat 2 tablespoons of oil in a wok or kadai on the highest flame available. When a drop of water flicked in the oil spits immediately, add the garlic-chilli paste and stir-fry for 30 seconds until fragrant and just turning golden at the edges.

Add the minced chicken, breaking it up immediately with a spatula into small pieces. Stir-fry on high heat for 4-5 minutes, pressing the mince against the wok surface occasionally, until no pink remains and the chicken begins to caramelise lightly at the edges.

Add fish sauce, oyster sauce, soy sauce and sugar. Toss and stir for 1 minute until the sauces coat every piece of chicken and reduce to a glossy glaze. Taste — it should be intensely savoury, slightly sweet and fiery.

Turn off the flame and immediately add the basil leaves. Toss them through with the residual heat — about 10-15 seconds is enough to wilt and perfume them without turning them black and bitter. Remove from heat.

In a separate small pan, heat 1 tablespoon oil over medium-high heat. Crack in the eggs and fry until the whites are set and crispy at the edges but the yolk is still runny, about 2 minutes. Spoon the basil chicken over individual bowls of steamed rice and top each with a fried egg. Serve at once.

substitutions.substitute: 300 g firm tofu, crumbled, for a vegetarian version
substitutions.notes: reduce stir-fry time to 3 minutes; the tofu should be pressed dry first

substitutions.substitute: 1 tbsp soy sauce plus 0.5 tsp sugar
substitutions.notes: approximates the sweet-savoury balance

substitutions.substitute: 1.5 tbsp soy sauce
20 fresh tulsi (holy basil) leaves
substitutions.notes: tulsi is botanically similar and gives an almost identical aroma

culturalNote: Pad Krapow is the dish Thais order when they cannot decide — sold from almost every street stall and cafeteria in Thailand as a reliable, fast and intensely comforting one-bowl meal. The fried egg on top (khai dao) is so expected that ordering without it is considered unusual.
regionalVariation: Pork (moo krapow) is the original street version; seafood krapow is popular in coastal cities. Northern Thai cooks sometimes stir a spoonful of red curry paste into the mince for extra depth and colour.
indianKitchenAdaptation: A cast-iron kadai on maximum gas flame replicates the wok perfectly. Minced chicken (murgh keema) is available fresh at any Indian chicken shop. Holy basil (tulsi) grows in most Indian homes and yards and is the closest plant to krapow basil — use it freely. Oyster sauce is found in the sauces aisle of most large Indian supermarkets; if unavailable, extra soy sauce with a pinch of sugar is a simple swap.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Thai Tom Yum Soup with Prawns
description: Thailand's electrifying hot-and-sour broth — a clear, spiced stock of lemongrass, kaffir lime and galangal made deeply aromatic, then loaded with plump prawns, mushrooms and ripe tomato. One spoonful is sharp, fragrant and warming all at once; it is the soup that defines an entire culinary tradition.

ingredients.name: lemongrass stalks, bruised and cut into 4 cm pieces
kaffir lime leaves, torn in half
galangal or fresh ginger, thinly sliced
ingredients.notes: galangal is traditional; ginger gives a slightly sharper broth

ingredients.name: fresh green or red chillies, bruised
lime or lemon juice, freshly squeezed
ingredients.notes: about 3-4 tbsp juice; add off the heat to preserve brightness

ingredients.name: roasted chilli paste (nam prik pao)
ingredients.notes: for a richer, smokier broth; substitute with 1 tsp extra chilli or omit

steps.text: Peel and devein the prawns, keeping the shells and heads aside. Place the prawn shells and heads in a saucepan with 1 litre of water and bring to a boil. Simmer for 8-10 minutes, then strain and discard the shells, reserving the sweet, amber-coloured prawn stock.

Bruise the lemongrass stalks firmly with a rolling pin or the back of a knife — they should be battered open to release their oils. Tear the kaffir lime leaves in half. These aromatics are not eaten; they are flavouring agents only.
Bring the prawn stock back to a rolling boil. Add the lemongrass, kaffir lime leaves, galangal or ginger slices and bruised chillies. Boil for 3 minutes to steep the aromatics and build the base.
Add the mushrooms and tomato wedges. Simmer for 3 minutes until the mushrooms are just cooked but still have a little bite. Add the roasted chilli paste if using and stir to dissolve.
Add the peeled prawns and cook for 2-3 minutes, just until they turn fully pink and opaque. Prawns overcook quickly — they should be just done, not rubbery.

Remove from heat. Add fish sauce and lime or lemon juice — both are added off the heat to keep their fresh, bright character. Taste: the soup should be intensely sour, salty and hot. Adjust by adding more lime for sourness, fish sauce for saltiness, or extra chilli for heat.
Ladle into bowls and top with fresh coriander and sliced spring onion. Warn diners that the lemongrass, galangal and kaffir lime leaves are for fragrance only and should be pushed aside when eating.

substitutions.substitute: 200 g fish fillets (tilapia, cod) or mixed mushrooms for a vegetarian version
2.5 tbsp soy sauce plus a pinch of salt
3 tbsp white vinegar (less fragrant but achieves the sourness)
substitutions.notes: fresh lime is always preferred

substitutions.substitute: 1 tsp grated lime zest added with the aromatics

culturalNote: Tom yum — literally 'boil and mix' in Thai — has been a household and restaurant staple for centuries, its broth as variable as the households that make it. The clear version (nam sai) showcases the aromatics cleanly; a tablespoon of coconut milk or evaporated milk stirred in at the end creates the creamier nam khon variant.
regionalVariation: Southern Thai tom yum includes more chillies and turmeric; in the north, the aromatics are heavier on galangal and lesser on lemongrass. Bangkok restaurants often serve it with straw mushrooms, which are sweeter than button mushrooms.
indianKitchenAdaptation: A heavy-bottomed patila or saucepan works well. Galangal can be fully replaced with fresh ginger (adrak), widely available everywhere. Lemongrass is sold at vegetable markets in larger Indian cities and increasingly on BigBasket; if unavailable, add a strip of lemon peel and a tablespoon of lemon juice at the start of cooking for a partial substitute. Fish sauce is available at gourmet stores and online; soy sauce is a quick swap.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Thai Pineapple Fried Rice
nativeTitle: ข้าวผัดสับปะรด
description: Fragrant jasmine rice stir-fried with sweet pineapple chunks, prawns, egg and cashews, perfumed with curry powder and fish sauce, and served inside a hollowed pineapple shell for a festive presentation. The natural sweetness of the fruit balances every savoury element in the wok.

ingredients.name: cooked jasmine rice, a day old and cold
fresh pineapple, cut into small chunks
curry powder or mild yellow curry masala

steps.text: If you have a whole pineapple, halve it lengthwise, scoop out the flesh (cutting around the core) and cut the flesh into small chunks, keeping the shell intact for serving. If using pre-cut pineapple, simply cut into 1.5 cm chunks. Cold leftover rice is essential — fresh rice steams and clumps; day-old rice fries and separates.

Heat a wok over high heat until it smokes lightly. Add the cashews to the dry wok and toast for 1-2 minutes until golden and fragrant, then remove and set aside. Return the wok to high heat, add 1 tablespoon oil and stir-fry the prawns for 2 minutes until just pink. Remove and set aside.

Add remaining oil to the wok. Add garlic and stir-fry 20 seconds until fragrant. Add the curry powder and stir for 10 seconds — it will bloom and turn the oil golden. Quickly add the cold rice and toss vigorously for 2-3 minutes, pressing and folding, until every grain is coated and heated through.

Push the rice to the sides. Add a little more oil to the centre, crack in the eggs and scramble loosely. When half-set, fold the egg through the rice and toss everything together until the egg is fully incorporated and the rice looks dry and separate.

Add the pineapple chunks, fish sauce, soy sauce and sugar. Toss everything together for 1 minute on high heat. The pineapple will release a little juice that the rice absorbs quickly. Add the cooked prawns back and toss once more.

Add the white parts of the spring onion and toasted cashews. Taste and adjust — it should be savoury, slightly sweet from the pineapple and warmly spiced. Serve in the hollowed pineapple shells or in bowls, garnished with coriander leaves and the green tops of the spring onion.

substitutions.substitute: 150 g chicken strips or extra cashews for vegetarian
substitutions.notes: chicken needs 4 minutes stir-fry time

substitutions.substitute: 2 tbsp soy sauce plus a pinch of salt
mango chunks or raisins for a different tropical note
roasted peanuts or slivered almonds
substitutions.notes: different texture but equally good

culturalNote: Pineapple fried rice served inside the fruit shell is a dish found across Thai resort towns and beachside restaurants, balancing the tropical abundance of the Thai coast with the wok's transformative heat.
regionalVariation: Southern coastal versions may include crab meat or squid; restaurant versions in Bangkok sometimes add golden raisins and a splash of fish sauce caramel for extra depth.
indianKitchenAdaptation: Use a cast-iron kadai or large non-stick pan at maximum heat. Day-old rice from your normal sona masoori or ponni stock works perfectly — the grain size is similar to jasmine rice. Curry powder (sold everywhere in India) replaces the Thai yellow curry spice blend closely. Fresh pineapple (annasi) is available at most sabzi markets year-round in tropical India. Cashews (kaju) are a natural Indian pantry staple.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Thai Mango Sticky Rice
nativeTitle: ข้าวเหนียวมะม่วง
description: One of Thailand's most iconic street desserts — glutinous rice steamed until tender, soaked in sweetened warm coconut milk and served alongside sliced ripe mango with a drizzle of salted coconut cream on top. Deceptively simple, deeply satisfying and completely vegan.

ingredients.name: glutinous (sticky) rice, soaked 4 hours or overnight
ingredients.notes: also called sweet rice or khao niao; soak before starting

ingredients.name: ripe alphonso or kesar mangoes
ingredients.notes: must be fully ripe and sweet

steps.text: Rinse the soaked glutinous rice until the water runs clear, then drain. Line a steamer basket with a damp muslin cloth or cheesecloth and spread the rice in an even layer. Steam over boiling water on medium heat for 25-30 minutes until the grains are translucent and tender but still slightly chewy — a grain pressed between fingers should be fully soft with no hard white centre.

While the rice steams, gently heat the coconut milk in a small saucepan over low heat. Add 3 tablespoons of sugar and 0.25 teaspoon of salt, stirring until dissolved. Do not boil — just warm enough to dissolve the sugar, about 3 minutes. Remove from heat and divide into two portions: roughly 300 ml for soaking the rice, and 100 ml reserved for the topping sauce.

Transfer the hot steamed rice to a large mixing bowl immediately. Pour the 300 ml portion of sweetened coconut milk over the hot rice and fold gently to coat every grain. Cover tightly with a plate or cling film and rest for 20-25 minutes — the rice will absorb the coconut milk and become glossy, soft and fragrant.

Make the salted coconut topping sauce: to the reserved 100 ml of coconut milk, add the remaining 1 tablespoon of sugar and 0.25 teaspoon of salt. Stir over low heat for 1 minute until the sugar dissolves. This thin, salty-sweet sauce is the finishing touch.

Peel the mangoes and slice the flesh cleanly off the stone in smooth, long cuts. Fan the slices out on a plate or arrange alongside a generous mound of the soaked sticky rice. Drizzle the salted coconut topping sauce over the rice, scatter toasted sesame seeds on top if using, and serve at room temperature. The dish does not keep well; eat within 2 hours.

substitutions.substitute: ripe papaya slices or fresh lychees
substitutions.notes: papaya is more widely available and equally complementary

substitutions.substitute: short-grain sushi rice for a slightly different texture
substitutions.notes: sushi rice works in a pinch; sticky rice gives a more authentic chewy result

substitutions.substitute: a drizzle of coconut cream and a mint leaf for garnish

culturalNote: Mango sticky rice (khao niao mamuang) follows the Thai mango season — April and May — when the fields of Chachoengsao and Nakhon Pathom flood the markets with fragrant nam dok mai mangoes. Street vendors push carts through Bangkok's lanes selling this dessert wrapped in banana leaves from April onwards.
regionalVariation: Northern Thai versions sometimes use black glutinous rice which gives a striking purple colour and a slightly nuttier flavour; the sweet coconut milk proportion and mango pairing remain the same.
indianKitchenAdaptation: Steam the rice in an idli steamer lined with a damp muslin cloth — this works excellently. Sticky (glutinous) rice is sold as 'sweet rice' or 'glutinous rice' in Indian Asian grocery stores and online (Amazon India). Indian alphonso, kesar or banganapalli mangoes in season are arguably better than Thai varieties and work perfectly. All other ingredients are readily available in India.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Thai Fish Cakes
description: Springy, fragrant discs of minced fish mixed with red curry paste, kaffir lime leaves, green beans and fish sauce, shallow-fried until golden and served with a sweet-tangy cucumber relish. A beloved Thai street snack that is crisp outside, bouncy within and packed with herbal aromatics.

ingredients.name: white fish fillets (tilapia, pollock or any firm white fish), roughly chopped
red curry paste (store-bought)
ingredients.notes: store-bought Thai red curry paste; mix of chilli powder and spices approximates it

ingredients.name: kaffir lime leaves, very finely sliced into thin strips
green beans, thinly sliced into 3 mm rounds
vinegar (white or rice)
ingredients.notes: for the dipping relish

ingredients.name: sugar (for relish)
ingredients.notes: for the cucumber relish

ingredients.name: peanuts, crushed (for relish)
ingredients.notes: sprinkled on the relish

steps.text: Make the cucumber relish first so it has time to meld: combine the diced cucumber with vinegar, sugar and a pinch of salt in a small bowl. Stir until the sugar dissolves. Add crushed peanuts if using. Set aside at room temperature.

Place the fish pieces in a food processor or mixie. Blend to a rough paste — you want it sticky but not completely smooth; some texture is good. Add the red curry paste, fish sauce, egg, sugar and half the kaffir lime leaf strips. Pulse a few times to combine.

Transfer the fish paste to a bowl. Add the sliced green beans and the remaining kaffir lime leaf strips. Mix well by hand — the mixture should be sticky enough to hold its shape when pressed into a patty. If too wet, refrigerate for 15 minutes.
With wet hands, shape the mixture into flat round patties about 6 cm wide and 1 cm thick. You should get about 12-14 pieces. Wet hands prevent sticking and help shape the patties smoothly.

Heat the oil in a flat-bottomed pan or tawa over medium heat. When the oil shimmers, add the fish cakes in a single layer without crowding. Fry for 3-4 minutes per side until deep golden and cooked through — press one gently with a spatula; it should feel firm, not squishy.
Drain on paper towel for 1 minute. Serve hot with the cucumber relish on the side for dipping. Fish cakes are best eaten immediately while crisp; they soften as they cool.

substitutions.substitute: 400 g peeled prawns, blended, for a prawn cake version
1 tbsp red chilli paste or a mix of 1 tsp chilli powder, 1 tsp lemongrass paste and 0.5 tsp turmeric
substitutions.notes: approximate the curry paste flavour

substitutions.substitute: 0.5 tsp finely grated lime zest mixed into the paste
finely sliced spring onion or grated carrot
substitutions.notes: for crunch and colour

culturalNote: Tod mun pla (literally 'fried fish paste') is one of Thailand's most popular street snacks, sold from carts alongside sticky rice and papaya salad. The bounce of a good fish cake — achieved by beating the paste until it becomes elastic — is a mark of the cook's skill.
regionalVariation: Southern Thai fish cakes are spicier and sometimes include turmeric for a yellow hue; versions in Isan use fresh river catfish and less coconut-based spicing.
indianKitchenAdaptation: Use a flat tawa or non-stick pan for even shallow-frying. Any firm white fish available in Indian markets works well — tilapia (tilapia), seer fish (vanjaram) or pomfret all give good results. Red curry paste can be approximated with a mixture of Kashmiri chilli paste, lemongrass paste (available as a tube in specialty stores) and a little ginger-garlic paste. A mixie blends the fish paste perfectly.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Thai Larb-Style Minced Chicken Salad
description: A vibrant, textured minced chicken salad from northeastern Thailand — cooked chicken mince tossed with fresh herbs, toasted rice powder, lime juice, fish sauce and chilli. Served at room temperature with a few sticky rice balls and raw vegetables on the side, it is as refreshing as it is fiery.

ingredients.name: minced chicken
ingredients.notes: murgh keema; ask for coarsely ground, not paste-fine

ingredients.name: raw rice, toasted and ground to powder
ingredients.notes: dry-toast in a pan until golden, then grind coarsely in mixie

ingredients.name: shallots or small red onions, thinly sliced
lemon or lime, juiced
ingredients.notes: about 4 tbsp fresh juice

ingredients.name: dried red chilli flakes
ingredients.notes: adjust to taste; larb is traditionally very hot

steps.text: Toast the raw rice in a dry pan over medium heat, shaking frequently, for 3-4 minutes until the grains are evenly golden and smell nutty. Let cool for 2 minutes, then grind coarsely in a mixie or mortar — it should have the texture of coarse sand, not fine powder. This toasted rice powder (khao khua) is what gives larb its distinctive textured bite.

Heat oil in a pan on medium-high heat. Add the minced chicken and cook, breaking it up constantly, for 6-8 minutes until fully cooked through and slightly caramelised. There should be no pink visible. Remove from heat and let it cool for 5 minutes.

In a large mixing bowl, combine the warm chicken mince with lime juice, fish sauce, sugar, chilli flakes and toasted rice powder. Toss well — the lime juice and fish sauce are the dressing, not a garnish, so mix thoroughly.

Add the sliced shallots, spring onion, mint leaves and coriander. Toss gently to combine everything without bruising the herbs. Taste: the salad should be lip-smackingly sour, salty, spicy and just barely sweet. Add more lime for sourness, fish sauce for saltiness or chilli for heat.

Pile onto a serving plate. Serve immediately with raw cucumber slices, cabbage wedges or a few balls of sticky rice on the side for wrapping. The herbs will wilt if left too long, so this dish must be eaten soon after tossing.

substitutions.substitute: minced pork or soya chunks (soaked and squeezed) for a vegetarian version
2 tbsp soy sauce plus a pinch of salt
4 tbsp tamarind water for a more tropical sourness
extra coriander or Vietnamese coriander (rau ram), if available

culturalNote: Larb (sometimes spelled laab or laap) is the national dish of Laos and equally beloved in northeastern Thailand (Isan), where it is eaten with sticky rice and is considered a dish of celebration — served at weddings, merit-making events and family gatherings.
regionalVariation: The raw version (larb dip) uses uncooked minced meat with more blood and extra chilli and is eaten in rural northern Thailand; the cooked version is more widely eaten and safer for home cooking. Pork larb is common in Laos; chicken is more typical of Thai restaurants.
indianKitchenAdaptation: Minced chicken (murgh keema) is available at every Indian chicken shop — ask for a coarse grind. A mixie or small food processor grinds the toasted rice to the right consistency in 10 seconds. All herbs — mint (pudina) and coriander (kothamalli) — are Indian pantry essentials. This salad requires no special equipment and comes together entirely in a kadai and a mixing bowl.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Vietnamese Chicken Pho (Pho Ga)
description: A pale, crystal-clear Vietnamese chicken broth perfumed with charred ginger, star anise and cinnamon, served over silky rice noodles with shredded chicken and a generous garnish platter. Lighter than the beef version, pho ga is a restorative bowl that proves fragrance is as important as flavour.

ingredients.name: whole chicken or 800 g chicken pieces with bone
fresh ginger, halved lengthwise
ingredients.notes: char directly over flame or dry-toast in pan for depth

ingredients.name: onion, halved
ingredients.notes: charred alongside the ginger for sweetness

ingredients.name: flat rice noodles (pho noodles)
ingredients.notes: soak in cold water 20 minutes, blanch 1 minute in boiling water before serving

ingredients.name: lime or lemon, wedges for serving

steps.text: Char the ginger and halved onion: hold them over a gas flame with tongs, turning, until charred in patches and soft, about 4-5 minutes. Alternatively, place cut-side down in a dry heavy pan over high heat until blackened. This step creates a sweet smokiness that is the signature of pho broth.

Place the chicken in a large pot with 2 litres of cold water. Bring to a boil over high heat. When the scum rises (about 5 minutes), pour off the water and rinse both the chicken and pot. This parboiling removes impurities and produces a clear, clean broth.

Return the cleaned chicken to the pot with fresh cold water (2 litres). Add the charred ginger, charred onion, star anise, cinnamon stick and cloves. Bring to a gentle boil, then reduce to the lowest simmer — the broth should barely murmur, not roll. Simmer for 50-60 minutes until the chicken is completely tender and the broth is fragrant.

Remove the chicken and set aside to cool slightly. Strain the broth through a fine sieve into a clean pot, discarding all solids. Season with fish sauce, sugar and salt. The broth should taste clear, savoury and deeply fragrant. Return to a gentle simmer.
When the chicken is cool enough to handle, shred the meat from the bones using your fingers or two forks. Discard skin and bones. Fluff the shredded meat and set aside.

Prepare the noodles: blanch the soaked rice noodles in a separate pot of boiling water for 1 minute until just tender. Drain and divide between 4 large deep bowls. Top each bowl with shredded chicken.

Ladle the hot broth over the noodles and chicken — it should be piping hot. Garnish with spring onion, coriander sprigs and bean sprouts. Serve immediately with lime wedges and sliced green chillies on the side so each diner can customise their bowl.

substitutions.substitute: 800 g mixed vegetables and tofu with vegetable stock for a vegan pho
substitutions.notes: use soy sauce instead of fish sauce

substitutions.substitute: 3 tbsp soy sauce plus a pinch of salt
fennel seeds (sombu), 1 tsp, for a milder anise note
substitutions.notes: the spice profile will be subtler but still aromatic

substitutions.substitute: any flat rice noodle or thin vermicelli; even leftover rice works as a rice soup variant

culturalNote: Pho originated in northern Vietnam in the early 20th century, likely as a fusion of French pot-au-feu and Chinese beef noodle soup influences, and has since become Vietnam's most internationally recognised dish. Pho ga (chicken pho) is lighter than the beef version and often served as a morning meal in Vietnamese homes.
regionalVariation: Hanoi-style pho is spare — fewer garnishes, clear broth, scallion oil; Saigon-style pho comes with a wide garnish plate of bean sprouts, fresh basil and hoisin sauce, and the broth is slightly sweeter.
indianKitchenAdaptation: Use a large stainless steel patila or pressure cooker (without pressurising it) for the broth — simmer with the lid ajar for the full time. Charring ginger and onion is easiest over an Indian gas flame using tongs. Star anise (annachi mokku) and cinnamon are Indian pantry staples. Flat rice noodles are sold in large Indian supermarkets; if unavailable, thin rice vermicelli (semiya made from rice) works. Fish sauce can be replaced with soy sauce.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Vietnamese Banh Mi Sandwich
description: Vietnam's iconic baguette sandwich — a crisp French-style roll split and layered with savoury pork patties, pickled carrot and daikon, cucumber, fresh coriander and a slick of chilli mayonnaise. The meeting of French colonialism and Vietnamese flavour is delicious, inexpensive and ready in 30 minutes.

ingredients.name: baguette or small French bread rolls (4 individual)
ingredients.notes: crusty outside, soft inside; cut lengthwise without breaking through the back

ingredients.name: minced pork or chicken (for pork patties)
sugar
ingredients.notes: 1 tbsp for pickling, 1 tbsp for meat

ingredients.name: vinegar (white or rice)
ingredients.notes: for quick-pickling the carrot

steps.text: Quick-pickle the carrot: toss the julienned carrot with 1 tablespoon vinegar, 1 tablespoon sugar and a pinch of salt. Massage briefly and let sit for at least 15 minutes — the carrot will soften slightly and turn tangy. This is the essential acidic note in banh mi.

Make the pork/chicken patties: combine the minced meat with garlic, soy sauce, fish sauce, 1 tablespoon sugar, black pepper and a pinch of salt. Mix well with your hands, then shape into 8-10 small flat patties or short sausage shapes.

Heat oil in a frying pan or tawa on medium-high heat. Cook the patties for 3-4 minutes per side until golden and cooked through with no pink inside. They should be slightly caramelised on the outside from the sugar in the marinade.
If the bread is not fresh and crusty, warm it in a dry pan, tawa or oven for 3-5 minutes until the crust is crisp. Split lengthwise without cutting all the way through, leaving a hinge at the back.

Assemble the banh mi: spread a thin layer of butter or oil on both cut surfaces of the bread. Layer in the cooked patties, drained pickled carrot, cucumber strips, generous coriander sprigs and sliced green chilli. Press gently to compact.

Drizzle with the remaining 2 tablespoons of vinegar mixed with a pinch of sugar as a light dressing — this mimics the classic daikon and carrot pickle brine. Serve immediately while the bread is still crisp.

substitutions.substitute: 200 g firm tofu, sliced and pan-fried, for a vegetarian filling
substitutions.notes: marinate with same soy sauce mixture

substitutions.substitute: any crusty roll, ciabatta, or even a thick slice of toasted white bread in a pinch
extra soy sauce for a fish-free version
lime juice for a brighter, more tropical pickle

culturalNote: Banh mi is the living culinary legacy of French rule in Vietnam — the baguette adopted and transformed by Vietnamese cooks who stuffed it with local ingredients, chilli, fish sauce and fresh herbs to create something entirely new and entirely Vietnamese.
regionalVariation: Hanoi-style banh mi is simpler, often just pork liver pate and butter; Saigon banh mi is loaded — multiple meats, pickles, herbs, cucumber and chilli sauce. Street vendors in Ho Chi Minh City serve hundreds a morning from a single cart.
indianKitchenAdaptation: French bread (baguette) is sold at bakeries in most Indian cities; pav (soft bread roll) can substitute in a pinch, though it is softer. A tawa works perfectly for pan-frying the patties. The quick-pickled carrot (gajar) with vinegar (sirka) and sugar is easy to make anywhere in India. Mince (keema) of chicken or pork is widely available; chicken keema at any Indian poultry shop is fresh and affordable.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Fresh Vietnamese Spring Rolls
description: Translucent rice paper wrappers filled with rice vermicelli, crisp vegetables, fresh herbs and tofu — assembled cold, never fried, and served with a punchy peanut-sesame dipping sauce. Light, crunchy and endlessly customisable, these are the perfect summer appetiser or make-ahead lunch.

ingredients.name: dried rice paper wrappers (22 cm round)
ingredients.notes: banh trang; sold in Indian Asian stores and online

ingredients.name: rice vermicelli or glass noodles, cooked and cooled
firm tofu, pressed and sliced into thin strips
lettuce leaves or fresh spinach, torn
ingredients.notes: any soft lettuce leaf or baby spinach works

ingredients.name: spring onions, cut into 5 cm pieces
peanut butter or roasted peanuts, ground
ingredients.notes: for the dipping sauce

ingredients.name: soy sauce
ingredients.notes: for the dipping sauce

ingredients.name: lime or lemon juice
ingredients.notes: about 2 tbsp juice, for dipping sauce

ingredients.name: sugar
ingredients.notes: for dipping sauce

ingredients.name: sesame oil or toasted sesame oil
ingredients.notes: a few drops add depth to the dipping sauce

ingredients.name: water
ingredients.notes: to thin the peanut dipping sauce to a pourable consistency

steps.text: Make the peanut dipping sauce: whisk together peanut butter (or ground roasted peanuts), soy sauce, lime juice, grated ginger, sugar and water until smooth. Add sesame oil if using. The sauce should be thick enough to coat a spoon but thin enough to drizzle. Taste and adjust lime for brightness and soy for salt. Set aside.

Cook the rice vermicelli in boiling water for 3-4 minutes until tender, then drain and rinse under cold water to stop cooking and prevent sticking. Toss with a few drops of oil. Slice the tofu into thin strips and prepare all vegetables into neat julienne or bite-sized pieces so the rolling is easy.

Fill a wide, shallow bowl with warm water (not hot — hot water makes the wrappers tear). Dip one rice paper wrapper into the water and swirl for 10-15 seconds until it just begins to soften and is pliable but still has slight resistance. Lay it flat on a clean damp surface — it will soften further as you fill it.

Place a lettuce leaf at the bottom third of the wrapper, leaving a 2 cm border. Layer a small amount of noodles, tofu strips, cucumber, carrot, a few mint leaves, coriander sprigs and spring onion pieces on top of the lettuce. Do not overfill — less is more for clean rolling.

Fold the bottom of the wrapper up over the filling. Fold in the two sides tightly, then roll forward firmly into a compact cylinder. The wrapper will stick to itself and seal. Repeat with remaining wrappers and filling, placing finished rolls seam-side down on a lightly oiled plate.

Serve immediately with the peanut dipping sauce. If not serving straight away, cover the rolls with a damp cloth to prevent the wrappers from drying out — do not refrigerate for more than 1 hour or the wrappers become tough.

substitutions.substitute: cooked prawn or shredded chicken for a non-vegetarian version
substitutions.notes: lay prawns decoratively near the top of the wrapper so they show through

substitutions.substitute: sunflower seed butter or tahini for peanut allergy
substitutions.notes: adjust seasoning of the dipping sauce

substitutions.substitute: if rice paper is unavailable, use iceberg lettuce leaves as wrapping — do not soak; just fill and fold
substitutions.notes: serves as a no-carb alternative

substitutions.substitute: a few drops of any neutral oil
substitutions.notes: sesame oil adds authenticity but the sauce is good without it

culturalNote: Goi cuon — fresh spring rolls — are a staple of Vietnamese home cooking and street food, light enough for the tropical climate and quick to assemble. They are distinct from the fried version (cha gio) that became famous internationally; fresh rolls are what Vietnamese families actually eat.
regionalVariation: Southern Vietnam (Ho Chi Minh City) tends to serve goi cuon with a peanut-hoisin dipping sauce; central Vietnam adds more herbs and a saltier dipping sauce called nuoc cham made with fish sauce, lime and chilli.
indianKitchenAdaptation: Rice paper wrappers (banh trang) are sold at Indian Asian grocery stores in Chennai, Mumbai, Bengaluru and Delhi, and widely available on Amazon India. If unavailable, soak idiyappam (rice noodle) rice and press into thin rounds — a close approximation. All vegetables and herbs are standard Indian market produce. The peanut dipping sauce uses groundnut butter (moongphali ka paste) which can be made fresh by blending roasted peanuts with a little oil.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Vietnamese Caramel Fish (Ca Kho To)
description: Fish fillets braised in a dark, sticky caramel sauce fragrant with ginger and fish sauce — one of Vietnam's most comforting home dishes. The sugar caramelises slowly to a deep amber that coats the fish in a sweet, salty, slightly smoky glaze. Served with steamed rice, it feeds a family on very little.

ingredients.name: firm fish fillets or steaks (tilapia, catfish, mackerel)
spring onions, cut into 3 cm lengths

steps.text: Pat the fish pieces dry with paper towel — surface moisture prevents caramelisation. Season lightly with salt on both sides and set aside.

Make the caramel: place the sugar in a small heavy saucepan or clay pot over medium heat. Do not stir — just swirl the pan occasionally. Watch carefully as the sugar melts and turns from golden to a deep amber, about 4-5 minutes. The moment it is dark amber (not black), remove from heat briefly.

Carefully add 100 ml of hot water to the caramel — it will sputter and steam dramatically. Return to heat and stir until the caramel dissolves into the water, then add fish sauce, minced garlic, ginger matchsticks and black pepper. Stir to combine.

Heat oil in a wide frying pan or clay pot over medium-high heat. Place the fish pieces in a single layer and sear for 2 minutes per side without moving them, until lightly golden. This builds flavour and helps the fish hold together during braising.

Pour the caramel sauce over the seared fish. Bring to a simmer over medium heat. Cook uncovered for 15-20 minutes, spooning the sauce over the fish every 4-5 minutes, until the sauce reduces to a thick, dark, glossy glaze that coats the fish. Taste — it should be deeply savoury and sticky-sweet.

Scatter spring onion lengths and sliced green chilli over the top in the last 2 minutes of cooking. Serve hot directly from the pot with steamed white rice — the dark braising liquid is the best part; spoon it over the rice.

substitutions.substitute: 400 g firm tofu steaks, pressed and dried, for a vegan version
substitutions.notes: reduce braising time to 10 minutes; caramel will still coat beautifully

substitutions.substitute: jaggery or palm sugar for a deeper, molasses-like caramel
substitutions.notes: watch closely as jaggery burns faster than white sugar

substitutions.substitute: 3 tbsp soy sauce for a fish-free version
substitutions.notes: the caramel will still be richly flavoured

culturalNote: Ca kho to is the defining dish of Vietnamese home cooking in the south — the clay pot (to) keeps the heat gentle and gives the dish a slightly smoky edge. Every Vietnamese mother and grandmother has their own proportion of caramel to fish sauce, and the dish is always slightly different depending on the kitchen.
regionalVariation: Northern Vietnamese versions use less sugar and more pepper; southern versions are sweeter. Some cooks add a splash of coconut water to the braise instead of plain water for additional sweetness and depth.
indianKitchenAdaptation: A heavy-bottomed kadai or non-stick pan works as well as a clay pot. Any firm fish available in Indian markets is perfect — mackerel (bangda), tilapia, surmai (seer fish) or catfish all braise well. Caramel-making is the only technical step; use white sugar (cheeni) over medium gas flame. The dish is a natural fit for the Indian palate — the sweet-savoury combination is reminiscent of Parsi and Goan sweet-sour fish preparations.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Vietnamese Lemongrass Chicken
description: A fragrant Vietnamese stir-fry of chicken thighs cooked with pounded lemongrass, garlic, chilli and a splash of fish sauce until the meat is caramelised at the edges and perfumed through with citrusy aromatics. One of the fastest and most flavourful dishes in Vietnamese home cooking.

ingredients.name: boneless chicken thighs, cut into 3 cm pieces
lemongrass stalks, white part only, finely sliced
fresh red or green chillies, sliced
turmeric powder
ingredients.notes: gives the chicken a golden colour

steps.text: Pound or finely mince the lemongrass into a near-paste using a mortar and pestle, blender or rolling pin — the finer the better, as coarsely chopped lemongrass is fibrous and unpleasant to eat. Combine with minced garlic and set aside.
Marinate the chicken: toss the chicken pieces with fish sauce, turmeric and sugar. Let sit for 10 minutes minimum while you prepare the aromatics.

Heat oil in a wok or kadai on high heat. Add the lemongrass-garlic paste and stir-fry for 1-2 minutes until fragrant and just beginning to turn golden. Add the sliced shallots and chillies and fry for another minute.

Add the marinated chicken in a single layer. Let it sear for 2-3 minutes without stirring so it picks up colour on the bottom. Then toss and stir-fry on high heat for 6-8 minutes until the chicken is cooked through with golden, caramelised edges and no pink inside.
If the pan looks dry, add 2 tablespoons of water and scrape up any caramelised bits — these are flavour. Taste and adjust: add more fish sauce for salt or a pinch of sugar if it needs balance.

Scatter spring onion rounds over the top and toss once. Remove from heat, garnish with coriander if using, and serve immediately over steamed jasmine rice. The dish should be dry-ish rather than saucy, with the lemongrass perfume prominent in every bite.

substitutions.substitute: 600 g firm tofu or soya chunks (soaked) for a vegetarian version
substitutions.notes: use soy sauce in place of fish sauce

substitutions.substitute: 2.5 tbsp soy sauce for a fish-free version
2 tbsp lemon zest plus 1 tbsp ginger, finely grated
substitutions.notes: not identical but gives a citrusy-gingery note

substitutions.substitute: 1 small red onion, thinly sliced

culturalNote: Lemongrass chicken is a Vietnamese staple that crosses regional boundaries — found from street stalls in Hanoi to home kitchens in the Mekong Delta. It relies on the combination of lemongrass and fish sauce that defines the Vietnamese flavour palette.
regionalVariation: Central Vietnamese versions are sharper and more chilli-forward with less sugar; the north uses more black pepper. Southern cooks sometimes add a tablespoon of coconut milk at the end for a slightly richer finish.
indianKitchenAdaptation: A kadai on high gas flame works exactly like a wok. Lemongrass (also sold as 'lemon grass' at Indian grocery stores and online on BigBasket) can be pounded in an Indian stone mortar (ammi). Turmeric (haldi) and all other spices are standard Indian pantry items. Boneless chicken thighs (deboned chicken) are available at most Indian butchers. This dish pairs well with Indian basmati or sona masoori rice.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Vietnamese Iced Coffee
description: Strong, dark Vietnamese drip coffee sweetened with condensed milk and poured over a tall glass of ice — one of the most popular drinks on earth for good reason. Intense, sweet, cold and deeply caffeinated, it is as much a mood as a beverage.

ingredients.name: ground dark roast coffee (Vietnamese robusta or strong espresso blend)
ingredients.notes: coarsely ground; Vietnamese Trung Nguyen or Bru is ideal; instant coffee works as backup

ingredients.name: sweetened condensed milk
ingredients.notes: 2 tbsp per glass; adjust to taste

ingredients.name: hot water (just off the boil)
ingredients.notes: 100 ml per glass; use 93-96°C water for best extraction

ingredients.name: ice cubes (generous amount)
ingredients.notes: about a full glass of ice per serving; keeps the drink cold without diluting immediately

ingredients.name: milk (optional, for a lighter version)
ingredients.notes: for ca phe sua nong (hot version), skip the ice and stir milk in after the condensed milk

steps.text: If you have a Vietnamese phin filter: place 2 tablespoons of ground coffee in the filter over a glass. Add 100 ml of hot water (about 95°C) and cover. Let it drip slowly for 4-5 minutes — the brew will be dark, intense and syrupy. If you do not have a phin filter, brew double-strength coffee by making a very concentrated French press or Moka pot coffee: use double the normal amount of coffee grounds.
While the coffee brews, place 2 tablespoons of condensed milk at the bottom of each glass. The condensed milk goes in first so it melts into the hot coffee rather than sitting on top of cold milk.
Pour the hot brewed coffee directly over the condensed milk. Stir briefly so the milk dissolves into the coffee. Taste — it should be intensely strong and very sweet; the ice will dilute it slightly.

Fill a tall glass generously with ice cubes. Pour the hot coffee-condensed milk mixture over the ice all at once — the contrast of hot coffee hitting cold ice creates a slightly frothy, chilled drink instantly. Stir once with a long spoon and serve immediately.

Drink slowly. The condensed milk settles and the ice melts gradually, subtly changing the drink's sweetness and strength as you go. This is part of the ritual. For the hot version (ca phe sua nong), skip the ice and serve in a small cup.

substitutions.substitute: 3 tsp sugar plus 2 tbsp cream or full-fat milk for a less sweet version
2 tsp instant coffee dissolved in 100 ml hot water for a quick version
substitutions.notes: flavour will be lighter but the method is the same

substitutions.substitute: coconut condensed milk for a dairy-free version
substitutions.notes: available online and at specialty stores

culturalNote: Ca phe sua da is Vietnam's most democratic drink — sold from tin-tray street carts, luxury hotel lobbies and every cafe in between. Vietnamese coffee culture runs deep: the phin filter is slow, meditative and yields a brew unlike any other in the world.
regionalVariation: Hanoi's coffee culture prefers the egg coffee (ca phe trung) — whipped egg yolk and condensed milk over black coffee, served in cold weather. Ho Chi Minh City's coffee scene is sweeter and more ice-heavy; coconut coffee (ca phe dua) is increasingly popular in the south.
indianKitchenAdaptation: South Indian filter coffee lovers will feel right at home — the double-decoction from a Moka pot or Bru/Nescafe strong brew makes an excellent substitute for Vietnamese drip coffee. Milkmaid (sweetened condensed milk) is at every Indian kirana store and supermarket. The only equipment needed is a kettle and two glasses. For the most authentic result, try Vietnamese robusta coffee brands (Trung Nguyen, G7) available on Amazon India.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Thai Stir-Fried Garlic Greens
nativeTitle: ผักผัดกระเทียม
description: The simplest Thai side dish — leafy greens stir-fried at scorching heat with plenty of garlic, oyster sauce and a splash of soy sauce until just wilted and glistening. Ready in 8 minutes, it is the dish every Thai home cook makes to round out a meal when the rice is almost ready.

ingredients.name: bok choy, pak choi or fresh spinach, washed and halved lengthwise
ingredients.notes: any leafy Asian green works; spinach (palak) is the closest Indian substitute

ingredients.name: toasted sesame oil
ingredients.notes: drizzled at the end for fragrance

steps.text: Wash and dry the greens thoroughly — excess water hitting a hot wok causes dangerous splattering and steams the vegetables instead of frying them. Cut large leaves in half or thirds lengthwise so they cook evenly.

Heat oil in a wok or kadai over the highest flame available. When the oil shimmers and just begins to smoke, add the sliced garlic. Stir-fry for 30 seconds until the edges turn golden but not brown — work fast to avoid burning.

Add all the greens at once and toss vigorously with a spatula and tongs. The greens will hiss, steam and reduce dramatically in volume — this is expected. Toss continuously for 2-3 minutes until they are just wilted and bright green, with the stems starting to become tender.
Add soy sauce, sugar and pepper. Toss for another 30 seconds to coat every leaf evenly. The greens should be glistening and bright; if they look dull or yellowish, they have been overcooked.

Drizzle the sesame oil over the top if using, toss once, and immediately transfer to a serving plate. Serve at once alongside rice and other dishes — stir-fried greens lose their vibrancy if they sit in the wok.

substitutions.substitute: broccoli, beans or cabbage — any firm vegetable can be stir-fried this way, adjusting time upward for firmer vegetables
coconut aminos for a soy-free version
a few drops of any toasted nut oil, or simply omit

culturalNote: Stir-fried greens with garlic are served as a side dish alongside rice and curry at virtually every Thai meal — the wok ensures the garlic perfumes the oil in seconds and the greens retain their colour and crunch.
regionalVariation: Restaurants in tourist areas often add oyster sauce for more depth; home cooks in rural Thailand rely on whatever greens the garden produces that day, from morning glory to sweet potato leaves.
indianKitchenAdaptation: A cast-iron kadai over maximum gas flame works identically to a wok for quick stir-frying. Fresh palak (spinach) is the most available and direct substitute for bok choy across India. Methi leaves (fenugreek greens) give a more bitter, complex result. Light soy sauce is widely available in Indian supermarkets. This dish is one of the easiest to reproduce in an Indian kitchen with zero specialty ingredients.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen
